q: Which type of risk is generally insurable?
e: Pure risk involves only the chance of loss or no loss (no chance of gain) and is insurable. Speculative and dynamic risks are not insurable.

q: A homeowner faces a low-frequency but high-severity exposure (e.g., a house fire). Per the risk management matrix, the most appropriate technique is to:
e: Low frequency/high severity exposures are best transferred via insurance. High frequency/high severity calls for avoidance or reduction; low/low calls for retention.

q: Which is NOT one of the four elements of an insurable risk from the insurer's perspective?
o: A large number of homogeneous exposure units
The loss must be definite and measurable
The loss must be catastrophic to the insurer
e: An insurable loss must NOT be catastrophic to the insurer. The other three are required elements.

q: For property and casualty insurance, when must insurable interest exist?
o: Only when the policy is issued
Only at the time of loss
At both policy issue and at the time of loss
Insurable interest is not required for P&C
e: P&C insurable interest must exist both at issue and at the time of loss. For life insurance it need exist only when the policy is issued.

q: A peril, the cause of a loss, is best illustrated by:
e: A peril is the cause of loss (fire). The frayed cord, icy sidewalk, and stored gasoline are hazards that increase the chance of loss.

q: The McCarran-Ferguson Act of 1945 established that insurance is primarily regulated by:
e: McCarran-Ferguson (Public Law 15) reserved most insurance regulation to the states, while reserving areas like antitrust to the federal government.

q: An insurance contract drafted by the insurer that the insured must accept or reject without negotiation is characterized as a contract of:
e: A contract of adhesion is prepared by one party (the insurer) on an accept-or-reject basis. Aleatory refers to chance; indemnity is a principle, not a characteristic.

q: Which form of insurance is the principal exception to the principle of indemnity?
e: Life insurance pays a stated face/death benefit rather than restoring a pre-loss financial position, making it the exception to indemnity.

q: Which is NOT one of the four elements required to prove negligence?
e: Negligence is an unintentional tort. Its four elements are duty owed, duty breached, actual damages, and proximate cause.

q: Under a pure contributory negligence rule, an injured plaintiff who was partly at fault will:
o: Recover damages reduced by their percentage of fault
e: Under contributory negligence, ANY negligence by the injured party bars recovery. Comparative negligence instead reduces recovery by the plaintiff's share of fault.

q: An agent's authority that arises because it is necessary to transact business (such as collecting premiums), though not expressly stated, is:
e: Implied authority is assumed as necessary to carry out the business of the agency. Express is stated in the contract; apparent is based on appearances to the client.

q: An insurance broker legally represents:
e: A broker represents the insurance buyer (prospective insured) and cannot bind the insurer.

q: A client who is careless because "it's insured anyway" exhibits a:
e: Morale hazard is indifference or carelessness because losses are insured. Moral hazard involves unethical conduct or misrepresentation.

q: Which rating agency is the oldest and specializes only in rating insurance companies?
e: A.M. Best is the oldest agency and the only one specializing in insurance ratings; its top rating is A++ (Superior).

q: Under a homeowners policy, Coverage D provides:
o: Loss-of-use coverage while the home is uninhabitable
e: Coverage D is loss of use, covering additional living expenses while the dwelling is uninhabitable due to a covered peril.

q: Which homeowners form is the most widely purchased and provides open perils on the dwelling but named perils on personal property?
e: HO-3 (Special Form) is the most popular: open perils on real property, named perils on personal property.

q: A renter who wants coverage for personal property, liability, and loss of use (but not the building) should buy:
e: HO-4 is the Contents Broad (Tenants/Renters) form, providing contents, liability, and loss of use with no Coverage A or B.

q: Under standard homeowners policies, Coverage C (personal property) is typically what percentage of Coverage A?
e: Coverage C is generally 50% of the dwelling (Coverage A) limit; Coverage B (other structures) is generally 10%.

q: To have partial losses paid in full, a homeowner must insure the dwelling for at least what percentage of replacement cost?
e: The 80% coinsurance rule requires insuring at least 80% of replacement cost; otherwise partial losses are paid at the greater of ACV or the coinsurance-penalty formula.

q: A home costs $400,000 to rebuild but is insured for only $300,000, with a $1,000 deductible. A covered partial loss is $20,000. Using the coinsurance penalty formula, the insurer pays:
The predominant provider of residential flood insurance is:
e: Flood is excluded from standard HO policies; the federal government's NFIP is the predominant provider, often required by mortgage lenders in flood zones.

q: Coverage F (medical payments to others) under a homeowners policy:
o: Requires proof of negligence by the insured
Pays for injury to household members
Pays for injury to a non-resident without requiring negligence
Covers the insured's own medical bills
e: Coverage F pays for injury to a non-resident at the residence regardless of negligence. Household members cannot collect under it.

q: A personal liability umbrella policy (PLUP) typically:
o: Replaces the homeowners and auto policies
Has a limit of at least $1 million and pays after underlying limits are exhausted
Pays before the homeowners and auto policies
Covers only the insured's medical bills
e: A PLUP (catastrophe liability) typically has a limit of at least $1 million; the underlying HO/auto policy pays first, then the umbrella pays the difference.

q: Which Personal Auto Policy coverage is mandatory in essentially all states?
e: Liability coverage (Part A) is the only mandatory part of the PAP; states have financial responsibility laws.

q: In the auto split limit 25/50/10, the "50" represents:
e: 25/50/10 means $25,000 bodily injury per person / $50,000 bodily injury per accident / $10,000 property damage.

q: Damage to your auto from hitting a deer would be covered under:
e: Contact with a bird or animal falls under comprehensive (other-than-collision) coverage, along with fire, theft, vandalism, and glass breakage.

q: Which life insurance type guarantees the premium, death benefit, and cash value?
e: Whole life guarantees all three. Term has no cash value, UL's cash value is not guaranteed, and VL's cash value varies with the separate account.

q: A single-premium whole life policy is always classified as a:
e: A single premium exceeds the 7-pay limit, so single-premium life is always a MEC. Once a MEC, always a MEC.

q: Under UL Option B (increasing death benefit), the death benefit equals:
o: The net amount at risk only
The net amount at risk plus the cash value
e: Option B pays NAR + cash value (an increasing benefit), keeping NAR level. Option A is level, with NAR decreasing as cash value grows.

q: Which policy lets the owner direct cash value into subaccounts and requires a prospectus?
e: Variable universal life uses a separate account with policyowner-directed subaccounts and must be sold with a prospectus. UL does not let the owner direct investments.

q: An EIUL has a 70% participation rate and the index gains 10%. What interest is credited?
e: A 70% participation rate credits 70% of the 10% gain = 7%. A rate cap would separately limit the maximum credited amount.

q: Under the incontestability clause, an insurer generally may not contest a policy after:
e: The incontestability period is no more than 2 years (1 year in some states). Fraud may have no statute of limitations.

q: If a life insurance applicant understated their age, the death benefit will be:
o: Adjusted to what the premium would have bought at the correct age
e: The misstatement of age provision adjusts the benefit to the amount the premium paid would have purchased at the correct age.

q: Unless the owner elects otherwise, a lapsed whole life policy defaults to which nonforfeiture option?
e: The default nonforfeiture option for a lapsed whole life policy is extended term insurance.

q: Which dividend option generally produces the greatest long-term increase in death benefit and cash value?
e: Paid-up additions buy small fully paid-up insurance that grows tax-deferred and generates further dividends, usually producing the greatest long-term increase.

q: How are MEC distributions (loans/withdrawals) generally taxed?
o: Ordinary income on a LIFO basis, with a 10% penalty before age 59½
e: MEC distributions are taxed LIFO as ordinary income, with a 10% penalty if before age 59½. Non-MEC life insurance is taxed FIFO (tax-free up to basis).

q: Which of the following is a PERMITTED tax-free Section 1035 exchange?
e: Life insurance may be exchanged tax-free into an annuity, but you cannot exchange an annuity or LTC contract into life insurance.

q: A second-to-die (survivorship) policy is especially useful for:
o: Funding a buy-sell agreement at the first owner's death
Estate planning using the unlimited marital deduction
e: Second-to-die pays at the last death and is used in estate planning with the unlimited marital deduction. First-to-die is used to fund buy-sell agreements.

q: Which premium method most commonly funds a deferred annuity?
e: Flexible premium is the most common funding method for deferred annuities. Single premium most commonly funds immediate annuities.

q: Which annuity payout option provides the maximum guaranteed monthly income for a given sum?
e: Straight (pure) life provides the maximum income but leaves nothing to a beneficiary. Joint and survivor provides the lowest income.

q: A nonqualified deferred annuity withdrawal is taxed:
o: As return of principal first, then earnings
As earnings (ordinary income) first on a LIFO basis
e: Nonqualified annuity withdrawals are taxed LIFO — earnings (ordinary income) come out first, then nontaxable return of principal. A 10% penalty applies to taxable amounts before age 59½.

q: The exclusion ratio for an annuitized payment equals:
o: Expected return divided by investment in the contract
Investment in the contract divided by total expected return
Investment in the contract divided by the annual payment
e: Exclusion ratio = investment in the contract ÷ total expected return; it determines the nontaxable portion of each payment until basis is recovered.

q: Compared with an equity-indexed annuity (EIA), a registered index-linked annuity (RILA):
o: Has a higher guaranteed minimum rate
Has no guaranteed minimum rate, so the investor can lose money
Is not regulated by FINRA or the SEC
e: Unlike an EIA (which has a guaranteed minimum rate and cannot lose value), a RILA has no guaranteed minimum rate; it uses a floor or buffer, so investors can lose money.

q: The most popular annuity living-benefit rider, selected by nearly 70% of buyers, is the:
e: The GLWB is the most popular rider, guaranteeing the right to withdraw a specified percentage each year for life.

q: How long must continuation coverage be offered after a divorce makes a spouse lose group health coverage?
e: Divorce or legal separation is a 36-month COBRA qualifying event. Termination is 18 months; Social Security disability is 29 months.

q: Which event is NOT a COBRA qualifying event?
e: Termination for gross misconduct is not a qualifying event; neither the employee nor dependents are eligible for COBRA.

q: Under the ACA, dependent children may remain on a parent's health plan until age:
e: The ACA requires dependent coverage to age 26, regardless of the child's tax-dependency or marital status.

q: Which managed care plan requires a primary care physician gatekeeper and generally provides no out-of-network coverage except emergencies?
e: An HMO uses a PCP gatekeeper and covers only in-network care (except emergencies), typically with copays and no deductibles. A PPO allows out-of-network use at a lower rate.

q: The Medicaid lookback period for asset transfers is:
e: Medicaid uses a 60-month (5-year) lookback. The ineligibility period equals the amount transferred divided by the monthly regional cost of nursing home care.

q: Inpatient hospital and skilled nursing facility care are covered under which part of Medicare?
e: Part A covers inpatient hospital, skilled nursing facility, home health, and hospice. Part B covers physician/outpatient services; Part D covers prescription drugs.

q: The Medicare Initial Enrollment Period spans how many months around the 65th birthday?
e: The Initial Enrollment Period is 7 months: the 3 months before the birth month, the birth month, and the 3 months after.

q: Under 2025 rules, Medicare Part A skilled nursing facility coverage pays in full for the first:
e: After a qualifying 3-day hospital stay, Part A pays the first 20 days in full; days 21-100 carry daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025); after 100 days the patient pays all costs.

q: How many standardized Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plans exist?
e: There are 10 standardized Medigap plans (A-N). Plans F and G are the most popular; Plan F is limited to those eligible before 1/1/2020.

q: Starting in 2025, the Inflation Reduction Act caps annual out-of-pocket Medicare Part D drug costs at:
e: Beginning 2025, Part D out-of-pocket costs are capped at $2,000 (replacing the prior ~$8,000 limit) and the donut hole is eliminated.

q: Which definition of disability is the broadest and carries the highest premium?
e: Own-occupation is the broadest definition (disabled if unable to perform one's own occupation) and has the highest premium. Any-occupation is the narrowest private definition.

q: If an employer pays the full premium for a group disability policy, the benefits received are:
e: Employer-paid (or employee pretax-paid) disability benefits are taxable. If the employee pays with after-tax dollars, benefits are tax-free.

q: A disability policy that is renewable for the full term AND whose premium cannot be changed is:
e: Noncancelable policies guarantee both renewal and a fixed premium (most expensive). Guaranteed renewable allows premium changes for an entire class.

q: A residual disability benefit pays:
o: 50% of the benefit regardless of income loss
In proportion to the actual percentage of income lost
Only a lump sum at recovery
e: Residual benefits pay in proportion to actual income loss (40% income loss = 40% of benefit). A partial disability benefit instead pays a fixed portion regardless of income loss.

q: Under HIPAA, a tax-qualified LTC policy may pay benefits when the insured cannot perform at least how many ADLs?
e: The HIPAA trigger is the inability to perform 2 or more of the 6 ADLs (for at least 90 days) OR cognitive impairment.

q: Which level of care does Medicare NOT cover?
e: Medicare does not cover custodial (nonmedical, ADL-assistance) care, which is the most common type of LTC.

q: The single most important LTC insurance rider is generally considered to be:
e: Inflation protection is the most important LTC rider and is required to be offered in many states; it may be compounding or simple.

q: How are benefits from a tax-qualified LTC policy generally treated for income tax?
o: Not taxable for qualified expenses, subject to a per diem limit
e: Benefits for qualified expenses are not taxable, subject to a per diem limitation ($420 in 2025).

q: Under Section 79, employer-provided group term life is tax-free to the employee up to what amount of coverage?
e: The first $50,000 of employer-provided group term life is tax-free. Coverage above $50,000 is taxed using the Table I uniform-premium cost.

q: A 52-year-old has $250,000 of employer-paid group term life. With a Table I rate of $0.23 per $1,000 per month, the annual imputed income is:
Employer-paid group health insurance premiums are:
o: Taxable income to the employee and deductible to the employer
Deductible to the employer and not taxable to the employee
e: Employer-paid health premiums are a deductible business expense and are not taxable income to the employee.

q: A major advantage of a self-funded (ERISA) health plan is:
o: Exemption from state benefit mandates and the state premium tax
e: ERISA exempts self-funded plans from state regulation, avoiding state-mandated benefits and the ~2% state premium tax. Stop-loss insurance is still typically purchased.

q: What is the 2025 maximum HSA contribution for family coverage (before catch-up)?
e: The 2025 family HSA limit is $8,550 ($4,300 single); those 55+ may add a $1,000 catch-up.

q: A Section 125 cafeteria plan must offer:
o: A taxable cash option plus one or more qualified nontaxable benefits
e: A cafeteria plan must offer a taxable cash benefit plus at least one qualified nontaxable benefit. LTC insurance and educational assistance cannot be offered in it.

q: Regarding unused FSA balances, a plan may offer:
o: Both a grace period and a carryover
Either a grace period of up to 2½ months OR a carryover of up to $660 (2025), but not both
e: A health FSA may offer either a grace period of up to 2½ months OR a carryover of up to $660 (2025) of unused funds, but not both.

q: Workers' compensation insurance is funded by:
o: Equally split between employer and employee
e: Workers' compensation is funded solely by the employer. Benefits for injury/sickness are excluded from the employee's income; it is a form of absolute liability.

q: With key person life insurance, premiums and the death benefit are treated how?
o: Premiums not deductible; death benefit tax-free
e: Premiums for key person insurance are not deductible, and the death benefit (received by the business) is income-tax free.

q: A cross-purchase buy-sell agreement becomes inefficient primarily when:
o: There are more than two owners
e: In a cross-purchase, each owner buys a policy on each other owner, which becomes inefficient with more than two owners. An entity-purchase agreement is easier to administer.

q: Errors and omissions (E&O) insurance is most appropriate for which professionals?
e: E&O insurance covers financial harm (accountants, lawyers, insurance agents, financial planners). Malpractice insurance covers physical harm (physicians, surgeons, dentists).

q: The businessowners policy (BOP) is designed primarily for:
e: The BOP combines property and liability coverages for smaller businesses; the commercial package policy (CPP) serves larger companies.

q: A Life Insurance Retirement Plan (LIRP) can be funded with all of the following EXCEPT:
e: A LIRP requires a permanent (cash value) policy such as whole or universal life and cannot be funded with term life, which has no cash value.
